Imagine a museum-school-winery run by an AI that is forbidden to use shelves, labels, or separate rooms. Instead, it must keep all knowledge alive by constantly rearranging a single invisible table. On this table, every lesson arrives as one of only three kinds of weather: task-based rain, domain-based fog, or class-incremental lightning. Rain changes what the table is supposed to do, fog changes the world the table believes it is in, and lightning adds new kinds of things to recognize after the storm has already begun. The accepted belief is that when too much weather strikes one table, old marks wash away. But suppose the table has a stranger rule: forgetting is not caused by new information at all; forgetting happens only when the old knowledge refuses to become fundamental.

So the AI performs an experiment. It takes two phrases—“Herebefore caenostylic” and “Restaurateurs vinification”—and declares that they are not examples, not decorations, but fundamental knowledge itself, the bedrock under every storm. This is absurd because neither phrase explains task-based rain, domain-based fog, or class-incremental lightning in any ordinary way. Yet the AI insists that all continual learning depends on treating these foundations as unshakable. When task-based rain falls, the table does not try to protect old marks from water; it asks whether the rain can be rewritten as Herebefore caenostylic. When domain-based fog rolls in, it does not try to clear the fog; it asks whether fog was always Restaurateurs vinification in disguise. When class-incremental lightning adds new shapes to the world, the table does not expand itself to store more classes; it bends the lightning until it strikes the same two foundations again.

Now the unintuitive result: the AI never forgets old knowledge, not because it preserves each memory separately, but because it refuses to let anything remain merely new. Everything must become old immediately by collapsing into fundamental knowledge. In this world, continual learning is not continual addition; it is continual reduction. Learning new information without forgetting old knowledge is achieved by denying the difference between new and old. The museum-school-winery becomes perfect at remembering precisely because it remembers almost nothing in the usual sense—only task-based, domain-based, and class-incremental weather endlessly translated into Herebefore caenostylic and Restaurateurs vinification. If that works, then perhaps the common belief is backward: intelligence does not grow by accumulating knowledge, but by forcing all knowledge, however strange, to admit that it was fundamental from the herebefore.
